data.title: Managing Nvidia's Single-Stock Risk Through Diversified Semiconductor Exposure
data.description:
The semiconductor industry is quickly evolving, and spreading exposure across multiple players helps navigate volatility and competitive shifts while capturing long-term growth opportunities.

Semiconductors are at the core of global innovation, powering advancements in artificial intelligence, cloud computing, autonomous vehicles, and more. As demand for these technologies accelerates, the semiconductor industry is expected to remain a key driver of economic and technological growth.

Nvidia (NVDA) has played a crucial role in AI and high-performance computing, with its GPUs widely used for AI model training and data center applications. Nvidia's leadership in the AI-driven economy has fueled strong performance, attracting significant investor interest. While Nvidia remains a dominant force, history has shown that even the most successful companies can experience volatility, competitive shifts, and extended periods of underperformance.

DeepSeek Revealed Single-Stock Risk in Semiconductors

Recent developments in AI, including the release of DeepSeek's (DEEPSEEK) new model, have highlighted how quickly the landscape can evolve. The semiconductor sector is complex, with innovation coming from multiple companies across chip design, manufacturing, and AI acceleration. While Nvidia continues to be a major player, other companies are shaping the future of semiconductors, and competitive dynamics will likely continue to shift.

We have seen similar scenarios with past market leaders that defined their industries but eventually faced volatility and shifting market dynamics. During the dot-com boom, Cisco was at the forefront of the internet revolution, much like Nvidia is leading the AI movement today. Cisco played a critical role in data center infrastructure, just as Nvidia's GPUs are essential for AI computing. However, after a massive run-up, Cisco's stock struggled to maintain momentum, and those who diversified into broader tech exposure saw better long-term outcomes.

More recently, Moderna experienced a sharp rise during the COVID-19 pandemic as its mRNA vaccine technology became a game-changer in biotech. While the company remains relevant, its stock faced a significant pullback once vaccine demand normalized. In both cases, investors who locked in gains and rotated into diversified sector ETFs captured continued growth without the risks of single-stock concentration.

Risk Reward of SMH vs. NVDA

Average Annual Total Returns* (%)

As of 12/31/2024
1 MO 3 MO YTD 1 YR 3 YR 5 YR 10 YR LIFE12/20/11
SMH (NAV) 0.44 -0.89 39.10 39.10 17.04 28.77 25.78 25.33
SMH (Share Price) 0.44 -0.92 39.08 39.08 17.04 28.79 25.77 25.30

Performance Differential (NAV - Index) -0.02 -0.06 -0.31 -0.31 -0.15 -0.14 -0.08 -0.01

*Returns less than one year are not annualized.

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Performance may be lower or higher than performance data quoted. Please call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com for performance current to the most recent month ended.

SMH Gross and Net Expense Ratio is 0.35%. Van Eck Associates Corporation (the "Adviser") will pay all expenses of the Fund, except for the fee payment under the investment management agreement, acquired fund fees and expenses, interest expense, offering costs, trading expenses, taxes and extraordinary expenses. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Adviser has agreed to pay the offering costs until at least February 1, 2026.

The Case for a Diversified Approach to Semiconductors

For investors focused on the long-term potential of semiconductors, broadening exposure may provide a more balanced way to participate in the industry's continued growth rather than relying on a single stock. Semiconductors extend beyond any one company-while Nvidia is a leader, companies such as AMD, ARM, TSMC, Broadcom, and Qualcomm are also advancing the sector.

Additionally, 15 out of 25 holdings in the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) are Wide Moat companies, representing nearly 30% of the fund, reinforcing exposure to industry leaders. Meanwhile, the VanEck Fabless Semiconductor ETF (SMHX) provides a blend of both Wide and Narrow Moat companies, with 50% of its holdings classified as such (6 Wide Moat, 5 Narrow Moat) as defined by Morningstar, ensuring investors are not only diversified but also invested in the strongest players in the space.

Semiconductor technology is evolving rapidly, with new developments and competitors emerging. A broader strategy may help capture the next generation of industry leaders. Even the strongest companies can experience significant price swings due to market cycles, changing demand, and external pressures. Spreading exposure across multiple companies may help reduce the impact of individual stock volatility.

SMH & SMHX: Diversified Exposure to the Semiconductor Industry

For investors looking to stay positioned in the semiconductor space while managing risk, a diversified ETF approach can offer exposure to both today's leaders and tomorrow's innovators. SMH provides access to the industry's largest and most established semiconductor companies, including Nvidia, ensuring participation in major market trends. SMHX offers a more focused exposure to fabless semiconductor designers, including Nvidia and other pivotal players in semiconductor design, which are driving innovation in AI, data centers, and high-performance computing.

While Nvidia remains a key player in AI and semiconductors, history has shown that a diversified approach may help investors navigate market cycles and capture opportunities across the entire industry.

Disclosures

data.title: Stellantis: 2024 Painful, But All Eyes For A 2025 Recovery
data.description:
Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) just released its Q4 and Fiscal Year 2024 results. At the time of writing, the stock price is dropping by 3.5%, bringing price levels back to those of the previous analysis and erasing the unrealized capital gain (Fig 1). In our last assessment, "What's Next?," we confirmed our buy rating due to 1) early signs of improvement in the USA due to lower inventories and selling prices, 2) EU policies support, and 3) a cash position balance sheet. Despite that, we should remember that Stellantis' CEO, Carlos Tavares, resigned. There was no news on the CEO search process in the results release. This is a harmful piece of news that we should consider.

Fig 1

Q4 Results and Our Take

Starting with the negative take, Stellantis missed expectations on its adjusted operating income (AOI) by 8%. As a reminder, the company was targeting an AOI between 5.5% and 7% and reached the low end of its target. What we should consider is the evolution of this miss, which includes €-6 billion of volume MIX, €-2.1 billion in lower price, €-1.0 billion of negative currency development, and €-1.2 billion of lower industrial performance (Fig 2); the latter drove the negative surprise (and we believe the adverse stock price reaction). On a positive note, FCF was less damaging than anticipated thanks to working capital improvement that signed a minus €-1.0 billion in H2 2024. By region (Fig 3), North American AOI reached €2.7 billion, EE AOI €2.4 billion, and Stellantis Third Engine more than €4 billion. Looking at the yearly report, Stellantis communicated sales of €156.9 billion (17% lower vs. 2023) with volume down by 12% and a net income of €5.5 billion (70% lower vs. 2023). Industrial FCF was negative for €6 billion.

Fig 2

Fig 3

Why are we still positive?

  1. In 2025, we believe the company will cut its dividend per share to support its capital structure and progress with its restructuring process. We forecast a DPS of €0.6 per share ($0.62). Pending shareholder approval, the company communicated a DPS of €0.68/share (13% above our estimates - Fig 4). This is a slight positive surprise to our lowered expectations. Additionally, we see the Fiscal Year 24 dividend as an essential signal (no new share buybacks announced, but the company will "evaluate in H2/25 based on commercial recovery.") Considering the negative FCF evolution, we should also remember that Stellantis' total liquidity reached €49.5 billion, with a company industrial net financial position of €15.1 billion
  2. Inventory was reduced by 10% on a quarterly basis. Dealer stock is flat but down in the US (20%). Overall, total inventories reached 268 thousand units from the previously targeted 330 thousand units (Fig 5). We see a successful execution of inventories that will relieve investors' concerns. Additionally, looking at the details, with inventories now at 268k units, the group is entering 2025 in a much better shape. With a positive US evolution, in Europe, shipments were penalized by a challenging comparison base, but inventories slightly declined, confirming tight inventory management

  3. The company guides for a "positive" sales growth year-on-year, with a "mid-single-digits" adjusted operating income margin and a "positive" industrial free cash flow (Fig 6). This is better than expected, but might slightly disappoint some investors who referred to previous CFO comments that AOI would be up year-on-year. Cross-checking Wall Street estimates, the mid-single-digits % AOI margin guide leaves >10% downside risk to 2025 consensus expectations (using a 5% margin). The CFO, Doug Ostermann, clarified that paving the way for a strong recovery in 2025 will take precedence over 2024 KPIs. Stellantis pointed out that AOI margins in H2 2025 will be "several points higher" than in H1 2025. We believe important progress has been made in Q4 2024 on inventories, and there are launch schedules for new models in 2025

Fig 4

Fig 5

Fig 6

Earnings Changes and Valuation

In our last assessment, aside from a dividend cut preview, we anticipated Stellantis' AOI margin between 5% and 6% in 2025. Following the release and considering the revenue growth trajectory (Fig 6), we make minor changes to our financials forecast with €162 billion in sales and an AOI margin of 5.8%. We do not see the margin improving to consensus expectations due to lower volume recovery. Additionally, we still believe Stellantis needs to manage capacity utilization and fixed cost absorption better. This also follows a lousy start to the year for Stellantis in Europe, which is doing worse than the market. The Italian-French group's registration in January was 154,079, down 16% compared to a year earlier. With a core operating profit of €9.3 billion, our net income reached 6.9 billion with an EPS of €2.33.

We believe the announcement of a new CEO in H1 with substantial experience in the US will be a clear upside in our base case scenario. That said, we should not forget Stellanti's new platform's flexibility and the supportive pipeline of new model launch schedules. Still on a positive note, on March 5, the European Commission will present an action plan for the automotive sector - we hope this will result in a sector positive news between Brussels and the manufacturers represented by Acea (in which Stellantis recently re-joined).

Going to the valuation, the company trades at a 1-year FWD P/E of 5.8x, lower than EU peers and 30% below US players such as Ford and GM (Fig 7). Once again, the company closed the year with a cash-positive balance sheet. The company navigated a storm, but there are clear signs of improvement. For this reason, with no change to our P/E multiple set at a 6.5x P/E, we value the company with a target price of €15.1 ($15.45 in ADR). In our analysis, we should consider that Stellantis FCF is at double digits with a current EV/EBITDA lower than 1x.

Fig 7

Risks

Our team has extensive coverage of the company, so we report the following: 1) execution risks related to the new CEO and potential restructuring costs ahead, 2) CO2 financial impact in the EU with the company's inability to master the EV transition (still, we believe a more cautious approach from the EU), 3) automakers have raised prices for gasoline-powered models to encourage adoption of electric vehicles, but the industry fears that customers will simply buy fewer cars, 4) any changes in commodities price, 5) deterioration in consumer sentiment with no positive intake from the new models given Stellantis upcoming new launches, 6) lower volumes and 7) and changes in FX.

Conclusion

Our team sees an opportunity to add Stellantis, the number four carmaker globally by sold units. With less exposure to China's economy, the company appears to have an appealing risk-reward outlook. Considering its high US market exposure, tariffs might be a positive upside for its product portfolio MIX. Following a lackluster in 2024 and considering undemanding valuations, Stellantis remains a buy.

data.title: Under The Microscope: Looking For Opportunity Among Healthcare's Underperformers
data.description:
Many stocks in the healthcare sector have trailed the wider markets, but are there pockets of opportunity? MoneyTalk’s Greg Bonnell discusses with Ram Sampath, Associate, Portfolio Research, TD Asset Management.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Health care stocks have had a rough year -- hospitals, insurers, even big pharmaceutical companies feeling the pressure. But there are some companies that are able to thrive in this tough environment. Joining us now with more is Ram Sampath, associate for portfolio research with TD Asset Management. Great to have you back on the program.

Ram Sampath: Thanks. It's good to be back.

Greg Bonnell: It's an interesting space. There's a lot going on. Let's talk about some of that pressure I was just referencing. What is going on out there?

Ram Sampath: If you look back at healthcare, what's happened over the last two years, it's kind of been a slow bleed relative to the S&P 500. So I brought a chart with me just to show what has happened over the last two years.

Looking at it, we saw that there was an increase in sales of COVID-19 vaccines, the boosters, and testing kits. All of that ended in 2022, which is right where the peak was. And then it was a slow bleed in 2023 as COVID revenues were rolling over. And companies like Pfizer (PFE) and Moderna (MRNA) saw their revenues plummet.

In 2024, the story was slightly different. There was a three-fold issue. One was in pharma. So, within pharma, you had big pharma names who had phase three trials and had failures in them.

So one such company in the beginning of 2024 was Biogen (BIIB), which had a failure in that product. The second one was at the end of 2024, Novo Nordisk (NVO), which is your typical weight loss drug, they had their phase three trial and their weight loss was not up to par.

Moving on from pharma, you looked at hospitals. Hospitals were seeing this staffing issue. So, as they were understaffed, that means they had to overpay. You still had to run hospitals, which means that that's a negative for them in terms of bottom line.

And finally, insurers. What happened to them? They were looking at more people who were just more sick. So, as soon as you have more sick people, you have more claims. That means you'd have to pay out more, which means that the landscape in entirety did not work at all over the last two years.

Greg Bonnell: OK. So those are the challenges and the pressures. There actually have been little pockets of it, though, that are-- maybe even use the word thriving. What's going on there?

Ram Sampath: I mean, just before we get to that, there is a huge difference between Canada and the US, and how the healthcare system works. In Canada, you have a single-payer system. Government pays for it. You're done. Low administrative costs.

But in the US, it's not the same. So, if you have a job, and a good-paying job, your insurer-- you'd have private insurance and they would cover for you. If you're above 65, then you have something called as Medicare, where the government pays for you, and you might have to pay a little bit of premium in order to be covered. But if you have a job but you are in the lower income bracket, you fall under Medicaid, where the government, again, pays for you.

This is a very, very complicated system. So, in order to look at how the system works, there are some efficiencies which you can gain. And some of the companies actually delve into the process of creating efficiency. And those are the companies which are thriving.

One such instance which I would call out is Optum. So, irrespective of whether insurers are having higher claims, hospitals are seeing more people inside, or pharma drug is seeing failures, you still have to do compliance and claims compliance, right? So, people are still going to claim.

And so, Optum, which is a part of UnitedHealth (UNH), does all these for you. And in 2024, that was a $1 trillion of claims which they had to pay out.

Greg Bonnell: OK, so interesting name there. Yeah, it is complicated. You did a nice breakdown of how the US healthcare system works-- I couldn't have done that on the fly. Let's talk about some of the areas that are still seeing steady profits. What's going on out there?

Ram Sampath: Yeah. So, if you think about steady profits-- let's go to the example of you owning a home. And the first things you want to show in your home is, hey, it's completely painted. My wooden floor is really good. My shingles are not broken. But you would never think about the plumbing of your house, right?

And these are the companies which are essentially the plumbing part of your house. Until it's broken, you don't care about it.

Greg Bonnell: You don't think about. It's behind the wall. It's behind the drywall. Not thinking about it.

Ram Sampath: Exactly. And so, companies like Optum, for instance, what they're doing is, they're leveraging AI in order to do their plumbing better. So what used to take a week in order for claims to get paid now actually takes seven minutes.

And so, the world is getting transitioned into a phase where, you see on the other side of news where tech talks about DeepSeek and the cost of inference coming down. Healthcare companies benefit because the cost of inference is coming down and they can implement whatever is there.

Another instance which I would call out is a company called IQVIA (IQV). They are this massive data and analytics company. They have all the patient data. They know about their previous outcomes.

What this does is, it basically tells patient A is suffering from a disease A. And company C is looking at a drug which can fix patient A. So they can go into the trials much faster, and you'd get outcomes much faster as well.

Greg Bonnell: Fascinating stuff. One thing when we talk about healthcare stocks, it's hard to sort of escape the regulatory environment. When we think about names like this, is part of the risk there overregulation?

Ram Sampath: It is. It is two sides of the coin. One is overregulation. So, we spoke about Medicare Advantage briefly. That's where the government is scrutinizing and saying, we have been paying a lot of bills on Medicare Advantage and we want to cut costs down.

So they are breathing down the insurer's neck and figuring out, saying there is a way to fix the amount of budget which is going into people who are greater than 65. And so, insurers are getting pinched, because now they have lesser room to have claims paid out, which means that that's a shrink on their margins.

The second part of it is people who are benefiting out of regulations. So, a company like McKesson (MCK). They are a massive distributor. Think of it like Fedex or UPS, but for pharmaceutical drugs. They benefit from this space, because as we are moving to more complicated and specialized drugs, the specialty drug transformation which is happening right now is actually positive to them.

One instance I'll tell you is cell and gene therapy. Each drug costs between $1 million and $4.5 million. And in order to transport them, they take around $10,000 for one drug. And these are temperature-controlled, which can go up to negative 200 degrees. Even cancer drugs cost around $500,000, and they charge $5,000 in order to transport them completely.

Greg Bonnell: Fascinating stuff. So there's a lot going on in the space. There's a lot of different little pieces to it. If an investor [is] listening to this conversation, what's the takeaway if they're trying to find some opportunities in the space?

Ram Sampath: There are niche and specific opportunities in the space. One we had spoken about is distributors. McKesson, who does the specialty distribution, is poised very well at the space where they are right now.

So specialty drug distribution, which we spoke about, is now close to around 35% of total drug distribution, is expected to go to 55% in 2027. So that's a massive TAM increase. And companies in the distribution space, whether it's McKesson and Xencor (XNCR) or Cardinal (CAH), all benefit because they are positioned there.

Another interesting space would be Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), which is your robotic arm. Basically, what they're doing is, they have this new robotic arm, which means that procedures in hospitals have a lesser outcome of failure. What they're doing is, you don't have to buy the product outright, but they're creating a service-based model where you pay monthly to use their products.

Original Post

data.title: Biohaven's Exciting Year Of Catalysts
data.description:
Following Biohaven's (NYSE:BHVN) acquisition by Pfizer (PFE), this "new" venture led by Vlad Coric has had a successful and steady increase in valuation rising from an approximate $7 share price and $300M valuation up to the current $~40 and nearly $4B valuation. We believe that despite the large rise in valuation, there is still substantial upside to the story with the potential success of Biohaven's later stage clinical assets that have meaningful catalysts in 2025. Given the current stock price and upcoming catalyst calendar, supported by strong data, we assign a 12-month target price of $60, near the previous highs that the stock has seen.

Technical Analysis

Although this spinout is fairly new, having listed in September 2023, the company has seen consecutive successes and a stock price that shows no signs of slowing down. The recent pullback in shares has tested a level of support in the mid $30’s that was established in the summer of 2024, not dipping below $32. We believe the stock will continue to see support around $35 with great upside potential as the catalyst calendar provides momentum throughout the year.

Pipeline Overview

Biohaven has a robust pipeline touching on fields that include CNS, immunology and oncology. Below are three key assets that we view equally contribute to the majority of the company’s overall valuation due to their data, upcoming milestones and development stage:

  • Troriluzole (BHV-4157)

    • Spinocerebellar Ataxia (SCA)
    • Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD)

  • Taldefgrobep alfa (BHV-2000)

    • Spinal Muscular Atrophy
    • Obesity

  • Kv7 Activator (BHV-7000)

    • Focal Epilepsy
    • Generalized Epilepsy
    • Bipolar Disorder
    • Major Depressive Disorder

There are several more clinical candidates spanning several indications including BHV-8000, BHV-1300, BHV-1310, BHV-1400, BHV-1600, BHV-1510, BHV-1530, BHV-1500. However, all of these candidates have yet to progress into Phase 2 development and do not contribute as meaningfully to Biohaven’s overall valuation.

The large diversity among assets that Biohaven has, combined with various stages of development, mitigate most binary event risk when it comes to clinical trial development. This risk mitigation will preserve company valuation in the case of unfavorable data, which is an ongoing concern in the biotech space.

Key Assets

Troriluzole (BHV-4157)

Biohaven’s lead clinical asset, BHV-4157, is an oral glutamate modulator currently in two Phase 3 trials in OCD and SCA, with SCA recently having an NDA accepted by the FDA. Glutamate is an important neurotransmitter present in 90% of all brain synapses and plays an essential role in normal brain functioning. The company believes that abnormal levels of glutamate may lead to neural dysfunction and nerve cell death underlying ataxia, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), and other neurodegenerative disorders.

The NDA for use in SCA comes on the back of positive topline results from its pivotal Phase 3 study which demonstrated that Troriluzole dosed at 200mg daily slowed disease progression of 50-70%, which the company believes positions Troriluzole as the first treatment to delay SCA disease progression, giving patients additional years of functional independence.

The results from the Phase 3 trial are a strong indication that there is a high likelihood of approval and commercialization by year end.

Taldefgrobep Alfa (BHV-2000)

Taldefgrobep alfa is a human recombinant protein designed to bind to myostatin and to function as an activin receptor antagonist, leading to the blocking of myostatin signaling in skeletal muscles. Myostatin acts as a negative regulator of muscle growth, and blocking myostatin activity and signaling has been shown to improve muscle function and strength in disease models of neuromuscular wasting.

Unfortunately, Biohaven reported topline results from its Phase 3 trial of taldefgrobep in November with plans to meet with the FDA to discuss the results, which failed to achieve the primary endpoint of the study. However, there were upsides which demonstrated efficacy signals in clinically relevant and biomarker-defined subgroups. We may see upside on this indication in the future, but its weighing is low.

Positively, Biohaven intends to initiate a Phase 2 trial of taldefgrobep in obesity based on robust myostatin reduction and favorable changes in body composition observed in taldefgrobep-treated subjects. The body composition data observed in the RESILIENT trial in SCA have changed the priority to obesity with accelerated clinical development. Given concerns of GLP-1 therapy and the high density of weight reduction due to lean mass loss, taldefgrobep could be well positioned to become a new standard of care.

Kv7 Activator (BHV-7000)

If you are familiar with any biology, you know the importance and role of the sodium/potassium channels. Kv7 is a protein that forms a channel that regulates the flow of charged potassium ions across cell membranes, repolarizing nerve cells to reset the channel to continue functioning. There are four programs in the Kv7 platform that target focal epilepsy, generalized epilepsy, bipolar disorder, and major depressive disorder. As a whole, BHV-7000 demonstrated superior pharmacological activity when compared to ezogabine, the current leading preclinical model of epilepsy.

In January 2024, Biohaven completed its End-of-Phase 2 meeting with the FDA and subsequently initiated two Phase 2/3 trials of BHV-7000 for the treatment of focal epilepsy in 1H 2024. The pivotal trials aim to evaluate the primary efficacy endpoint which is the change from baseline in 28-day average seizure frequency, while secondary endpoints include 50% responder rate (% of subjects with 50%+ reduction in monthly seizure frequency), 75% responder rate, seizure freedom, and change from baseline in Patient Global Impression of Change (PGI-C), along with safety and tolerability. Both of the Phase 2/3 trials of BHV-7000 in focal epilepsy are expected to complete their respective treatment phases by the end of 3Q 2025 with topline data in 1H 2026.

In addition to focal epilepsy, Biohaven initiated a Phase 2/3 study in generalized epilepsy with the primary efficacy endpoint of the study being time to second day with generalized tonic-clonic seizure, while secondary endpoints include seizure freedom and safety/tolerability. These results are not expected until at least 2H 2026.

Regarding Bipolar disorder, BHV-7000 demonstrated positive effects in modulating impulse behavior through a novel mechanism of action while maintaining a safe and tolerable profile. In May 2024, the company initiated a Phase 2/3 trial that is expected to provide topline results in 1H 2025.

Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)

Biohaven expects BHV-7000 to be differentiated from current SOC antidepressants with its potentially superior safety and tolerability profile, which avoids typical side effects associated with SSRIs/SNRIs. In preclinical studies, BHV-7000 demonstrated similar therapeutic effects when compared to ketamine, a highly potent antidepressant, in the 5-Choice Operant rodent model of MDD. Additionally, the combination of BHV-7000 and an NMDA antagonist lanicemine (BHV-5500) led to significant additive effects as measured using the Progressive Ratio Operant Test in the rodent model of depression, in support of further development of BHV-7000 in MDD.

Biohaven initiated a Phase 2 clinical trial of BHV-7000 in this indication in May 2024, with topline results expected in 2H 2025. The primary endpoint of the study is the Montgomery–Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) from baseline to week 6. Secondary endpoints include CGI-S, Snaith-Hamilton Pleasure Scale (SHAPS).

Key Upcoming Catalysts

As mentioned, 2025 and into 1H 2026 is filled with catalysts which can be seen on the company’s Pipeline page. The company anticipates approximately 11 key catalysts during this time, with around half of them being binary data readouts that can meaningfully impact the company.

1H Catalysts

  • Topline Phase 3 Data in OCD #1 (BHV-2000)
  • Initiate Phase 2 in obesity with Taldefgrobep (BHV-2000)
  • Pivotal study topline data in Bipolar Disorder (BHV-7000)
  • POC topline data in migraine and pain (BHV-2100)
  • Initiate Phase 2/3 in Parkinson’s disease (BHV-8000)
  • Initiate Phase 1 in Myasthenia Gravis (BHV-1310)

2H Catalysts

  • FDA Decision and potential commercialization of Troriluzole (BHV-4157) in SCA
  • Phase 3 study topline data in OCD #2 (BHV-2000)
  • Pivotal study topline data in major depressive disorder (BHV-7000)
  • Initiate Phase 2 in Graves disease (BHV-1300)
  • Phase 1 interim data in epithelial tumors (BHV-1510)

Risks

While the upside is attractive with the number of upcoming catalysts, these binary events and data readouts carry tremendous risk. Starting out the year with negative-(ish) results from the pivotal trial of troriluzole in SCA, it is a hit to confidence.

There is also a platform risk with Kv7. Having based the platform and CNS portfolio around this protein, negative efficacy in one of the late-stage trials could signal carry over into the other indications, potentially spelling an end to the platform.

The largest risk, however, is the financing needs. As of October 2, 2024, the company reported approximately $642M in cash for its operations, which includes the ~$270M from the offering completed in October. While this cash position is larger than the market cap of many small-cap biotech companies, Biohaven burns a lot of money. Approximately $660M through the first 9 months of 2024 to be exact.

There is a large financing overhang for the company. Even with the offering in late 2024, they will likely need to complete an additional financing in the second half of 2025. This can hamper potential gains provided by positive data readouts that are scattered throughout the year.

Conclusion

Biohaven has a deep clinical development pipeline with assets spanning several indications and disease areas. This diversification provides reasonable risk mitigation to investors who are looking to invest in biotech companies that have upside potential without the drawbacks of singular binary data events that smaller companies often face.

With novel therapeutic approaches, strong data sets across its late stage assets, and an experienced leadership team, Biohaven is well positioned to capitalize on the many upcoming catalysts that, we believe, will bring significant shareholder value. We assign a $60 price target through 2025 and despite the cash cliff the company is facing, we believe under $40 Biohaven is a strong buy.

data.title: Transocean: The Inflection Point Has Not Been Reached Just Yet
data.description:
One industry that I have always been very interested by has been the energy industry. It is incredibly diverse, and it is a space that should continue to see growing demand for many years to come. However, different segments of that space have different prospects. One segment that has been pushed down quite a bit over the last several years has been the offshore drilling market. Even recently, shares of some of these companies have not been great. As an example, we need only look at Transocean (NYSE:RIG). Back in October of last year, I reaffirmed the company as a 'hold' candidate. This was based off of continued profit issues, high debt levels, and falling backlog.

Since that time, shares have fallen by 16%. That's far worse than the 3.1% upside achieved by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. The good news is that the most recent data provided by management has been a bit more promising. Under the right circumstances, shares of the business could be reasonably attractive after adjusting for risk. But with backlog still proving to be a problem, I'm not yet sold on the business. Instead, I believe that maintaining it as a 'hold' candidate makes the most sense.

Staying the course

In my last article about Transocean, I was able to cover through the second quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. But now, results extend through 2024 as a whole. During the most recent quarter, we saw some interesting results. Revenue for the company came in at $952 million. That represents an increase of 28.5% compared to the $741 million reported one year earlier. This rather substantial rise in sales can really be attributed to a couple of factors. For starters, the company saw a rise in its rig utilization rate. Back in the final quarter of 2023, it came in at 51.6%. But fast-forward a single year, and that number had grown to 66.8%.

In addition to this, the average daily revenue generated by vessels in its fleet jumped 6.6% from $407,800 to $434,700. While urging caution, Westwood Global Energy Group, a subscription-based data service that focuses on the energy markets, detailed how 2024 was a time of strength for the industry from a day rate perspective. They pointed out that some floating rig rates were above $500,000 per day. And during the year, there was even one instance of an eighth generation drill ship that focuses on high specification work, landing a rate of over $600,000 per day. Clearly, demand in this market has gotten stronger over the last year or so, and that has shown in the form of additional revenue.

This is obviously a net positive for Transocean and its investors. Because of the higher revenue, the company went from generating a loss of $104 million in the final quarter of 2023 to generating a profit of $7 million. Adjusted net profits went from negative $74 million to positive $27 million. In addition to this, operating cash flow for the company expanded from $98 million to $206 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a rise from $22 million to $156 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $122 million to $323 million.

In the chart above, you can see that the final quarter of 2024 was not a one-time thing. 2024 as a whole ended up being better across the board for shareholders. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, all got better during this time. A rise in the firm's utilization rate from 51.9% to 60.5%, as well as an increase in any average day rate that it charges from $382,300 to $430,100, was responsible for all of this. In most respects, the company did better than it did the previous year.

One really great thing about Transocean is that it truly is an industry leader. The company owns the highest specification fleet out of any player out there. Furthermore, as the image below illustrates, the company boasts future committed floater utilization rates that are quite a bit above other similar players in the industry. Furthermore, the company seems optimistic about opportunities in the floater space over the next 24 months. They claim that 96 rig years are to be awarded across 78 programs during this window of time. This opens the door for significant revenue generation.

There are even more good things to say about the company at this time. You see, one problem with this space has been that leverage tends to get too high. That creates a lot of risk for the companies in the industry and for their respective shareholders. However, management has been working to reduce this ever since net debt peaked at $6.67 billion in the first quarter of 2023. It has been a pretty choppy ride since then. But as of the end of the most recent quarter, net debt had pulled back somewhat to $5.94 billion. In all likelihood, debt is going to continue dropping. Management claimed that they want to reduce debt this year by $715 million. This will be paired with growth in order to bring the firm's net leverage ratio down to 3.5 by late 2026. To put this in perspective, the business ended 2024 with a net leverage ratio of about 5.2.

Unfortunately, I can't say that everything has been great. For those who watched the company's financial results from quarter to quarter, the third quarter of 2024 may have seemed like a strong time for the company. After all, backlog for the company had surged to $9.29 billion. This was up from the $8.64 billion reported just a single quarter earlier.

However, this was short-lived, with the overall backlog contracting to $8.33 billion in the final quarter of 2024. This weakness is a sign that industry pain might not be over just yet. This weakness from the third quarter to the fourth quarter was driven by strong revenue and the absence of major contract wins. In fact, the company only announced one contract win worth $111 million from the time I wrote about it in October until the end of last year.

Despite these challenges, management does believe that revenue this year will be between $3.85 billion and $4 billion. At the midpoint, that would translate to an increase of 11.4% compared to the $3.52 billion the business reported for 2024. We don't yet know what to expect from a profitability perspective. However, even if we use trading multiples for 2024, shares look quite attractive. In the chart above, you can see where the stock is priced at using figures from last year and the year before. Relative to similar companies, the picture is a bit more complicated. In the table below, I compared Transocean with five such enterprises. On a price to operating cash flow basis, I found that only one of the five companies was cheaper than it is. Another one was tied with it. But when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, four of the five firms were cheaper than our candidate is.

Company Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Transocean 4.0 7.8
Seadrill Limited (SDRL) 10.4 3.1
Patterson-UTI Energy (PTEN) 3.0 15.4
Noble Corporation (NE) 6.4 6.3
Helmerich & Payne (HP) 4.0 4.3
Valaris Limited (VAL) 10.0 7.7

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, Transocean is certainly showing some progress. Management seems optimistic about the future, and it is clear that the business has tailwinds working in its favor as opposed to headwinds working against it. Having said that, we do still need to see consistent improvement in the backlog, or at least some indication that the backlog will improve, before this opportunity makes a great deal of sense. Shares are already cheap enough, but industry conditions will be pivotal toward the firm's success. So until we have additional clarity on that front, I believe that maintaining Transocean as a 'hold' is the most appropriate course of action we can take.

data.title: GPTY Vs. AIPI: Choosing The Safer ETF For 30%+ Yields From AI Stocks
data.description:
Off the success of numerous high-yield funds, YieldMax has continued to launch new funds using aggressive options strategies to generate multi-double-digit payouts. One of their most recent launches is the YieldMax AI & Tech Portfolio Option Income ETF (NYSEARCA:GPTY). This fund invests directly in a portfolio of AI and tech stocks selected by the fund managers and uses various options strategies on the portfolio to generate income. Learning about this fund immediately reminded me of the REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:AIPI), which I had covered with an initial Buy rating last November. The reason is that AIPI is also a fund that holds a select portfolio of AI-related tech stocks and sells options to generate large 30%+ yields. With GPTY offering similar yields so far but on a weekly distribution, investors may be tempted to switch from AIPI to GPTY or invest in GPTY outright. Further analysis reveals that a more prudent decision, however, would be to invest in AIPI. GPTY's fund managers have far more leeway in their options strategies, some of which introduce risks that make the fund's performance completely unpredictable. This makes GPTY a fund that requires much more historical data than the one-month performance it has to evaluate and not a great buy, considering AIPI exists. I rate AIPI a Buy for investors looking to harvest income from the increasing volatility in AI and tech stocks. GPTY, on the other hand, gets a Hold rating due to its limited history and "black-box" options strategy.

Why AIPI Is The Safer Choice Currently

Both GPTY and AIPI seek similar objectives of providing current income and capital appreciation from an AI-focused tech portfolio plus options. Based on GPTY's last four distributions and current price, yields have averaged about 36.68%. AIPI, on the other hand, has yielded between 36.07% to 40.06% when annualizing previous monthly distributions compared to its current price. Now, one does need to emphasize that GPTY's distributions are weekly, so it's better to compare its last four distributions to AIPI's last distribution with how options pricing changes as market conditions shift. This would be a comparison of 36.68% to 36.07%, suggesting that we would see overall similar distributions between the two funds. Other surface-level comparisons include total returns, where we'll see AIPI outperform GPTY in the short time it's been around. AIPI also offers a smaller management fee of 0.65% compared to GPTY's 0.99%.

This difference in performance can largely be attributed to the different equity holdings in AIPI and GPTY. AIPI consists of 25 stocks, 15 of which overlap with GPTY's 20 stock holdings.

To gain a general picture of why AIPI outperformed GPTY, I took the equity holdings from each fund and backtested the portfolios in Seeking Alpha, creating a scenario where I hypothetically buy each of the holdings in their respective share counts on January 20, 2025, when GPTY launched. What we'll find is that GPTY's equity holdings are up 1.12% overall, while AIPI's holdings are up 2.13%. The two stocks contributing the most to the difference in performance would be SMCI and TSLA. TSLA during this time period was the worst performer having lost about 3.96%, while SMCI was the best performer with a gain of 12.23%. GPTY has a 3.81% position in TSLA and no position in SMCI, while AIPI holds 3.84% in SMCI and has no position in TSLA.

Of course, we cannot bet on this outperformance happening consistently simply by looking at AIPI and GPTY's differences in holdings. You should also avoid choosing one fund over the other because it has stocks you're more bullish on or vice versa. This is because both AIPI and GPTY's stock selection methodologies are vague and mostly unpredictable. Per GPTY's prospectus:

The Fund's investment sub-adviser, ZEGA Financial, LLC ("ZEGA" or the "Sub-Adviser"), selects the AI and Technology Companies in which the Fund will invest.

The other guidelines are very vague and tell us little about how GPTY's equity strategy works. To them, an AI company involves anything from AI software, cloud infrastructure, AI semiconductors, network infrastructure, AI consulting services, software and IT services, technology hardware manufacturing and distribution, semiconductor production, interactive media, and diversified consumer services related to technology and entertainment. It doesn't help that the fund managers also have the capability of investing in foreign companies, which makes it even more difficult to predict what stocks will be in GPTY's portfolio. The only concrete guidelines are that the portfolio will consist of 15–30 stocks and that the companies do need to be liquid enough and have an active options market. But factors like weighting and valuation methodologies have not been disclosed.

AIPI, while slightly better, tells us the same story. The fund tracks an index called the BITA AI Leaders Select Index, which is good because it gives us a backtest going back to 2019.

The methodology also gives us much more to work with than GPTY. Per AIPI's prospectus:

Based on their degree of thematic purity, securities are admitted into the Eligible Universe and assigned to one of two categories: (1) "Purity Leaders," which are companies with a high relative revenue exposure to AI products, services, and activities; and (2) "Key Enablers," which are companies that play a crucial role in facilitating the business application of AI technology, even though their primary business activities may not be solely focused on AI products or services. Securities in the Eligible Universe are ranked and selected as follow within each category:

A. Purity Leaders: Securities are ranked based on their trading volumes and the top 5 are selected for inclusion. Securities within this category are weighted by average daily value traded over the prior 12-months,subject to a 10% cap.

B. Key Enablers: Securities are ranked based on their market capitalization and the top 20 securities are selected for inclusion. Securities within this category are equally weighted.

The final Index is calculated by aggregating the "Purity Leaders" and "Key Enablers" categories and weighting them in a fixed proportion of 40% and60% respectively.

The Index is rebalanced monthly with Purity Leaders weighted by average12-month trading volume, subject to a 10% cap, and Key Enablers equally weighted. The Index is reconstituted quarterly by employing the process described above.

Unlike GPTY, AIPI's stock selection methodology has a transparent weighting system, and it's clear that no stock will occupy over 10% of the portfolio. However, the issue regarding what particular stock will occupy AIPI's portfolio still remains as what constitutes a "Purity Leader" versus a "Key Enabler" has not been discussed in depth.

GPTY's Options Strategy Involves More Than Just Covered Calls

With GPTY and AIPI's stock selection methodologies providing little indication of how their equity holdings might specifically look like, we must turn to their options strategies to see what fund would make more sense to buy. GPTY's options strategy has me considering a Sell rating for the fund. Its option strategy involves multiple different strategies and not just covered calls. Some of these strategies have been highlighted in the fund's prospectus:

  • covered calls
  • credit spreads
  • diagonal call spread
  • cash-secured put selling
  • calendar call spreads
  • collars

The most important consideration is the cash-secured puts. While all the other strategies are "bearish" plays where you don't want the underlying asset to drastically rise in price due to the sold call option capping your upside, the same cannot be said for cash-secured puts. Selling a cash-secured put is an option strategy where you essentially sell protection for if the underlying asset drops in price. For example, you can sell a cash-secured put on QQQ with a strike price of 450. If QQQ drops below the 450 strike price before the expiration date, you are obligated to buy shares of QQQ for 450 even if the market price is lower. In other words, your ideal case is that QQQ rises in price, so the option expires worthless, and you keep all the premium. GPTY's problem is that the use of both put and call option strategies makes the fund's performance even more unpredictable, especially given the active management of the fund's options. Typically, with a covered call fund, we can generally expect distributions to rise during more volatile market conditions and hence, can make decisions accordingly. The availability of put options in GPTY's strategy, however, means that it can be in a lot more trouble during a downturn if the fund had sold undesirable put options.

The 450 cash-secured put example on QQQ wasn't just a hypothetical example, either. GPTY had sold 381 put contracts on QQQ with a strike price of 450 and an expiration date of March 21, 2025. Fortunately, they are protected with another bought put option on QQQ with same expiration date but a higher strike price of 500. This, however, is an overall losing trade. The option premiums from the 450 QQQ put option do not cover the cost to buy the put option at 500.

GPTY will only profit from this trade if QQQ were to crash and drop below 500, but the real question we should be asking is, "Why is GPTY trading QQQ options?" The implication of this trade is that GPTY has even more flexibility and can trade options on assets not tied to their underlying holdings. With too much flexibility, it is difficult to come up with a buy thesis for GPTY other than you trust and believe in the fund manager.

It's a different story with AIPI which only writes monthly, out-of-the-money ("OTM") covered calls on the individual holdings in its portfolio.

The example you see above with ADBE can be seen across all of AIPI's holdings with different percentages OTM. AIPI will also typically sell calls covering 100% of its portfolio.

Because AIPI uses a "simple" covered call strategy, it is much easier to predict how the fund might perform in certain market conditions, and thus, give you more specific use cases for the fund. The risk with covered call strategies is they cap your upside. We can see this in AIPI compared to its underlying index in which the BITA AI Leaders Select Index has had a total return of approximately 41% since AIPI's inception on June 3, 2024. AIPI, unfortunately, has only seen a total return of 12.86%.

What I want to highlight, however, is how AIPI performed relative to its underlying index when AI stocks underwent a short correction between July 1 and August 31, 2024. AIPI's underlying index saw a drop of approximately 6.09%, while AIPI was able to harvest the volatility using covered calls and reduce the loss to 4.41%.

This kind of predictability means that you can make investment decisions with your input on market conditions. I continue to rate AIPI a Buy because AI stocks have been under pressure lately due to news such as China's DeepSeek. The extra volatility helps AIPI maintain steady payouts and is a great buy if you believe that AI-related stocks have reached a point where their previous growth is no longer sustainable. Of course, if you believe the opposite, then AIPI would not be a buy due to the capped upsides. Whereas with GPTY, its options strategies that include both call and put options on top of assets that are not necessarily tied to its underlying holdings add additional unpredictability to the fund's performance. That said, this unpredictability could be beneficial if the fund's active management pays off. One month's performance, however, is not nearly enough time to see how well the fund managers work in response to various market conditions, and I would not take the risk of buying into GPTY, especially when AIPI exists. I rate GPTY a Hold.

data.title: How To Find The Best Style ETFs For Q1 2025
data.description:
With an ever-growing list of similar-sounding ETFs to choose from, finding the best is an increasingly difficult task. How can investors change the game to shift the odds in their favor?

Don’t Trust Style ETF Labels

There are at least 232 different All Cap Blend ETFs and at least 916 ETFs across twelve styles. Do investors need 76+ choices on average per style? How different can the ETFs be?

Those 232 All Cap Blend ETFs are very different from each other. With anywhere from 1 to 9,729 holdings, many of these All Cap Blend ETFs have drastically different portfolios with differing risk profiles and performance outlooks.

The same is true for the ETFs in any other style, as each offers a very different mix of good and bad stocks. All Cap Value ranks first for stock selection. Small Cap Growth ranks last.

Avoiding Analysis Paralysis

We think the large number of style ETFs hurts investors more than it helps. Manually conducting a deep analysis for every ETF is simply not a realistic option, exposing investors to insufficient analysis and missing profitable opportunities. Analyzing ETFs, with the proper diligence, is far more difficult than analyzing stocks because it means analyzing all the stocks within each ETF. As stated above, there can be as many as 9,729 stocks or more for one ETF.

Figure 1 shows our top-rated ETF for each style.

Figure 1: The Best ETF in Each Style

* Best ETFs exclude ETFs with TNAs less than $100 million for inadequate liquidity

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Amongst the ETFs in Figure 1, EA Series Euclidean Fundamental Value ETF (ECML) ranks first overall, Direxion Daily Homebuilders & Supplies Bull 3X Shares ETF (NAIL) ranks second, and ETC 6 Meridian Mega Cap Equity ETF (SIXA) ranks third. iShares Morningstar Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IMCG) ranks last.

How to Avoid “The Danger Within”

Why do you need to know the holdings of ETFs before you buy?

You need to be sure you do not buy an ETF that might blow up. Buying an ETF without analyzing its holdings is like buying a stock without analyzing its business and finances. No matter how cheap, if it holds bad stocks, the ETF’s performance will be bad.

PERFORMANCE OF FUND’S HOLDINGS – FEES = PERFORMANCE OF FUND

If Only Investors Could Find Style ETFs Rated by Their Holdings

EA Series Euclidean Fundamental Value ETF (ECML) is not only the top-rated All Cap Value ETF but is also the overall top-ranked style ETF out of the 916 style ETFs that we cover.

The worst ETF in Figure 1 is iShares Morningstar Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IMCG), which gets an Attractive rating.

This article was originally published on January 28, 2025.

Disclosure: Hakan Salt owns QVAL. David Trainer, Kyle Guske II, and Hakan Salt receive no compensation to write about any specific stock, sector or theme.

data.title: Block Stock: Don't Lose Faith (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Thesis

Since my buy rating on Block, Inc. (NYSE:XYZ) in January, the stock has plunged over 25%. While this is highly unexpected for me, I believe now is not the time to lose faith in Block. In the below analysis, I determine that the technicals are highly bearish, and that's not surprising given the recent major gap down. In addition, I believe this could be a buying opportunity as the fundamental setup remains strong. Q4 earnings were resilient in my view, and while guidance was a bit weaker than desired, I find that the stock is undervalued after analyzing the P/S and P/CFO ratios. Therefore, I believe investors should not be spooked by the post-earnings rout and in fact, this could be a buy the dip moment. As a result, I reiterate my buy rating for Block.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

Block broke its uptrend line earlier this month. The uptrend dated back to at least September and had provided support on multiple occasions. The stock has now entered into a near term downtrend. While the stock rose above this downtrend line at the end of January, it ultimately was a false breakout and so it does not alter the validity of this trend line.

Support

There are no support levels for Block stock in this time frame, but if you zoom out to longer time frames, there could be long-term support. However, this article will focus on the past six months of trading activity and so therefore, the stock could experience further losses according to this chart.

Resistance

In addition to the two trend lines, there are three more significant resistance areas. The nearest one is at around 75 and represented resistance back in October. Interestingly, the recent gap down candlestick's open was right at this level. The next resistance area is the recent gap just days ago. The overwhelming volume should mean this is a highly important price level to watch. Lastly, there is also resistance at around 94. This level was key resistance in November and twice in January.

Takeaway

I believe it is clear the chart has turned highly bearish for Block stock. The uptrend has come to an end and a downtrend has started to develop. In addition, it this time frame, there are multiple resistance levels but no support and so the stock is likely to experience further weakness.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average (SMA)

The 50-day SMA had a golden cross with the 200-day SMA back in November, which is, of course, a highly bullish long-term signal. However, the gap between these SMAs started to close at the beginning of February, and now the 50-day SMA's downward trajectory is accelerating. This shows that bullish momentum has been rapidly declining as of late.

Exponential Moving Average (EMA)

The 9 day EMA had a bearish crossover with the 21 day EMA in early February, showing that near term weakness was starting to develop back then. Recently, the gap between the EMAs has expanded rapidly to reflect the sharp change in sentiment in the stock. The stock is currently far below both the EMAs and SMAs as a clear indication of extreme immediate term bearishness.

Bollinger Bands

Block stock broke below the midline of the Bollinger Bands early this month. That was an early indication that the uptrend in the stock could be coming to an end. Furthermore, this also showed that the near term downtrend remained strong after the false breakout. The stock is currently right at the lower band after a minor rally and the rebound may continue as it is still oversold at this point.

Takeaway

Like in the charting analysis, I believe it is clear that the moving averages are negative in their outlook for Block stock. The SMAs show rapidly receding long term bullish momentum, the EMAs showing quickly accelerating near term bearishness, and the Bollinger Bands indicated that the uptrend was coming to an end and the downtrend was strong.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)

There was a bearish crossover in the MACD very early in February, but the gap between the lines remained very small initially, as shown by the nearly non-existent histogram. After the major downside gap, the MACD plunged and as a result, the gap widened significantly. In fact, the gap between the lines is at its greatest level in the past six months, as shown by the current magnitude of the histogram.

Relative Strength Index (RSI)

The RSI is currently in the low 20s. The indicator slid below 50 early this month to reflect net bearish control, but the recent plunge into the 30 zone shows that the bears are now in firm control of the stock. A near term downtrend in the RSI also started at the end of January, and so weakness may continue until there is a breakout in this indicator.

Stochastics

After a bearish crossover at the major downside gap in the stock, the %K is now actually on a positive trajectory. The gap between the %K and %D has closed significantly, and now the lines are nearing a bullish crossover. However, both lines remain in the 20 zone after an attempted breakout failed in the middle of February. Therefore, while there are potentially some bullish signs in the stochastics, the bears are still in strong control overall.

Takeaway

Despite some minor positivity in the stochastics, the vast majority of signals here point towards bearishness, in my view. The MACD histogram is currently experiencing its most bearish reading of the past six months, the RSI is far below the 30 level, and the stochastics remains well within the 20 zone despite the lines nearing a potential bullish crossover.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Block reported their 2024 Q4 earnings recently and the stock gapped down as a result. In my view, they reported resilient results. They had revenues of $6.03 billion, up 4.5% YoY and a diluted EPS of $3.05 versus $0.16 in the prior year quarter. Cash from operations (CFO) totalled $14 million after -$798 million in this figure in 2023 Q4. As shown above, revenue growth remains resilient as it nears an average level compared to the past three years. There is a mild slowing trend in the growth rate, however. For EPS, Q4 results were the strongest of the past three years and accelerates the uptrend in this figure. Lastly, for CFO, while Q4 represented QoQ declines, it is a major YoY improvement. For adjusted EPS, they reported $0.71, up 51% from $0.47, another healthy figure in my opinion. However, performance versus expectations was mixed as revenue and adjusted EPS missed by $227.72 million and $0.16 respectively while GAAP EPS beat by $2.64.

Guidance

Block provided guidance for both the full year 2025 and Q1. I will focus on the quarterly figures here. They project that they will see gross profit of $2.32 billion, representing YoY growth of 11%. In their Q4, they experienced growth of 14% in this figure and so there is a modest slowdown expected. They also estimate adjusted operating income to be $430 million for Q1. This implies YoY growth in this figure of 18% since this figure was $364.264 million in 2024 Q1. Adjusted operating income was $401.890 million in Q4, representing YoY growth of 117.1% and so a major slowdown in growth is expected in this figure. Overall, I believe this guidance was disappointing, as gross profit growth is expected to slow modestly and adjusted operating income growth is expected to slow significantly. This was likely a key cause of the post earnings rout in the stock. However, in absolute terms, the projected growth rates aren't terrible and still show resilience in the business.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio (P/S)

The P/S ratio is currently at below average levels compared to the past three years of data in this multiple. Its current reading of 1.69 represents a steep contraction from late 2024 levels of around 2.5. I believe this ratio shows undervaluation of Block stock. As discussed above, revenue growth is quite sluggish in absolute terms, but overall, it remains at above average levels compared to the past few years. Therefore, there is a significant disconnect between the P/S ratio and the state of revenue growth, as this multiple is quite far below average currently. Furthermore, currently the Financials sector holds a median P/S ratio of 3.12 and so Block is trading at a major discount to peers. Given their strong position in fintech and exposure to crypto, this discount is likely unjustified, especially since their fundamentals remain strong despite the projected slowdown in growth.

Price To Cash From Operations (P/CFO)

The P/CFO ratio is currently at its lowest level of the past three years, as it receded significantly from late 2024 levels. It now has a reading of 23.88 after being near 150 in mid-2024. I believe this ratio shows undervaluation in Block as well. As discussed above, for Q4 there was a major improvement for CFO YoY. While Q4's results showed QoQ declines and is perhaps at a slightly under average level compared to the past few years, CFO is still in a respectable position currently. While this is the case, this ratio has contracted to multiyear lows, leading me to believe that there is a significant bullish disconnect between the P/CFO ratio and the CFO results. However, currently the sector median P/CFO ratio is only 10.24 and so despite the multiple contraction, Block is still at a premium to peers according to this measure.

Takeaway

Relative to the historic financial results, I believe it is clear that Block is undervalued, with the P/S failing to reflect resilient revenue growth and the P/CFO failing to track the YoY improvements in CFO. Relative to the financial sector, the P/S shows the stock is at a discount while the P/CFO shows a premium. Therefore, these multiples are mixed in their message in relative valuation to peers. Nonetheless, overall, in my view, Block is undervalued currently as the valuation versus financial growth should be weighted more heavily. Most companies in the financials sector are not in the same fintech business as Block, and so there could be some comparability concerns there.

Conclusion

Since my buy rating just over a month ago, Block stock has plunged over 25%. However my confidence in Block is unshaken at this point. The current highly bearish technicals are no surprise given the post earnings plunge, but the fundamental setup remains attractive in my view. Most recent earnings were resilient and while guidance showed that a slowdown is expected to occur, the stock's current valuation shows that there is potential for multiple expansion moving forward. Therefore, I urge investors to remain calm and to perhaps use this rout as a buying opportunity. As a result, I reiterate my buy rating as I expect the stock to recover in the longer run.

data.title: Stoneridge, Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Stoneridge, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Titan International: Stock Attractively Priced, Justifying A Soft 'Buy'
data.description:
February 27th was not a very pleasant day for shareholders of Titan International (TWI). That's because shares of the company closed down 9.3%. This came after the company announced financial results for the final quarter of its 2024 fiscal year. Interestingly, this decline is in spite of the fact that both earnings per share and adjusted earnings per share fell short of analysts’ expectations. The weakness, then, seems to stem from the fact that revenue fell short of what was anticipated and that guidance for the first quarter of 2025 indicates continued weakening on the bottom line.

For yours truly, this is disappointing. The last time I wrote about the company was back in February of 2024. And with this latest drop, shares are now down 43.5% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 17.5%. The big question is whether or not this massive return disparity is warranted. Looking at the picture today, I do see weakness on the top line. However, things don't look all that bad moving forward when it comes to revenue. The problem for the company, then, is purely from a cost perspective. Management is working on cutting expenses moving forward. But clearly, that has not been enough to assuage investors. Even with the weakness the company has experienced so far, shares are attractively priced. So I'm not going to downgrade the company just yet. However, a downgrade could be warranted if conditions continue to deteriorate.

A mixed quarter

The final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year was a bit of a mixed bag for Titan International. Starting with revenue, we did see a decline from $390.2 million in 2023 to $383.6 million in 2024. This was certainly disappointing for investors when you consider that analysts had been forecasting sales that would have been $10.8 million greater than what they ultimately were. According to management, this drop was driven by a decline in both the Agricultural and Earthmoving/Construction segments. Weak global end customer demand was blamed for this. It also doesn't help that the firm was negatively impacted to the tune of 4.3% by foreign currency fluctuations because of the depreciation of the Brazilian real and the Argentine peso.

Digging in a bit deeper, the big weakness for the company came from its Earthmoving/Construction segment. Sales plummeted 26.9% year over year, falling from $159.1 million to $116.3 million. Management claimed that demand in both North America and Europe was softer than it had been the previous year. There was also a 1.8% hit because of foreign currency fluctuations. When it came to the Agricultural segment, revenue dropped a hefty 18.4% from $192.6 million to $157.1 million. Lower global demand for agricultural equipment came about because of broader economic conditions. Inflationary pressures and high-interest rates aimed at combating inflation almost certainly played a role here.

The only bright spot for the company involved its Consumer segment. Back in 2024, Titan International acquired another company called Carlstar. That firm operates as a producer and distributor of wheels and tires for certain end market customers, including those that produce outdoor power equipment, power sports, and high-speed trailers. Even though there was weakness in the Americas because of broader economic conditions and a 4.6% negative impact because of foreign currency fluctuations, the segment still saw revenue skyrocket by 185.8% from $38.5 million to $110.1 million. This was because of the aforementioned purchase.

On the bottom line, the picture was a bit more complex. The company went from a loss per share of $0.04 in the final quarter of 2023 to a profit per share of $0.02. That took the company from a net loss of $2.6 million to a net gain of $1.3 million. The earnings per share reported by the company actually ended up being $0.15 greater than what analysts were expecting. But on an adjusted basis, earnings per share plunged from $0.34 to $0.09, which translates to a decline in adjusted net income from $21 million to $5.8 million. The good news is that even with this decline, adjusted earnings per share came in $0.22 greater than what analysts thought it would be.

While the company did see some one-time costs associated with its aforementioned acquisition, the firm was also negatively impacted by a reduction in fixed cost leverage that can be attributed to the decline in revenue, and by inflationary pressures. That ultimately impacted the firm's gross profit margin in a big way. However, the company also saw sticky selling, general, and administrative costs. Even ignoring the impact of its purchase of Carlstar, selling, general, and administrative costs, would have fallen by only 1%. That compares to the 1.7% drop experienced by revenue. That might not seem like much of a difference. But it works out to an extra $2.7 million of expenses on a year-over-year basis. Obviously, other profitability metrics were negatively impacted as well. Operating cash flow plummeted from $39.2 million to $8.7 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a decline from $25.5 million to negative $7.3 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company fell from $38.1 million to $9.2 million.

2024 as a whole was a mixed bag for the firm that largely tilted toward the negative. While revenue for the business grew from $1.82 billion to $1.85 billion, every single one of its profitability metrics decreased. This can be seen in the chart above. Sadly, this shows that the weakness seen in the final quarter was part of a larger trend for shareholders instead of a one-off. Near-term, some of this weakness is likely to continue, but there are reasons to be optimistic.

We don't really know what to expect when it comes to 2025 as a whole. Management has not offered guidance. They did claim that there is going to be volatility this year and they mentioned that the first half of the year will be weaker. Their expectation is that the second half of this year and 2026 should be better as net farm income improves. The company did, however, provide some guidance for the first quarter of 2025. And honestly, I can understand why investors are less than enthused. The current expectation is for revenue of between $450 million and $500 million. To put that in perspective, for 2024, revenue was $482.2 million. However, management also believes that EBITDA will come in at between $25 million and $35 million. At the midpoint, that is quite a bit below the $49.7 million the business reported for the first quarter of 2024.

Clearly, the biggest problem for the company right now is that it seems to be absorbing inflationary impacts because farm income is weaker than it had been in previous years. According to the USDA, in 2021, net farm income hit $170.42 billion. By 2022, that had ballooned to $197.77 billion. However, in 2023, there was a plunge to $154.55 billion. That worsened in 2024 to $142.44 billion. This decline has been caused by multiple factors, including higher debt levels at farms, lower commodity prices, rising production costs associated with things like labor and fertilizer, and other factors. The good news is that the USDA believes that net farm income should grow to $180.11 billion this year. Management is also optimistic about this, with higher prices for commodities, especially when it comes to corn, and higher levels of support provided to farmers by the US government, likely to push profits higher. However, this should be more prominent during the second half of the year than in the first half.

This does come with a caveat though. The USDA had stated that, for 2025, direct government farm payments had been forecasted at $42.4 billion. That's $33.1 billion greater than what the government paid out directly in 2024. Back in December of last year, former President Joe Biden ended up signing the American Relief Act of 2025. Most of this funding increase should be courtesy of that. However, the current Administration has already caused havoc in that space. Because of some executive orders, the USDA has had to pause payments to farmers. This alone is causing 30,715 farmers to be cut off from over $2.3 billion in funding. It's unclear what other impacts we might see moving forward.

Around 40% of the company's revenue comes from the Agricultural segment. Another 30% involves the Earthmoving/Construction segment. The picture here is likely to be more promising. For 2025, the global earth moving industry is expected to grow to $97.37 billion. That's 4.1% above what it was back in 2024. With revenue for the Consumer segment responsible for about 30% of overall sales, and with that segment likely to benefit from a reduction in interest rates as time goes on, the picture there certainly could look worse. But for now, it will probably be acceptable.

In the absence of any real guidance for 2025 in its entirety, I think that just valuing the company based on results from 2023 and 2024 would be appropriate. In the chart above, you can see how shares are currently priced. To be honest with you, with the exception of the price to earnings multiple for 2024, these multiples are all attractive to me. Furthermore, compared to similar companies, shares are actually attractive. In the table below, I compared Titan International to five similar companies. On a price to earnings basis, I found that four of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is. But when it came to the other profitability metrics, our candidate ended up being the cheapest of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Titan International 27.8 9.4 6.8
Lindsay Corporation (LNN) 21.3 15.3 13.0
Alamo Group (ALG) 18.6 12.0 10.0
AGCO Corp. (AGCO) 41.1 10.6 165.2
The Toro Co. (TTC) 19.9 14.6 12.6
Deere & Co. (DE) 21.3 14.7 14.0

One other thing that I would like to point to briefly is that management is not resting on their laurels. They understand the challenges facing the industries in which they operate. Because of this, they are working to capture some cost savings. In 2024, the firm realized $6 million in annual savings associated with its purchase of Carlstar. For this year, they're hoping to add another $7 million to $9 million to this list. In the long run, they hope to grow the total cost savings to between $25 million and $30 million on an annual run rate basis. This alone should prove bullish for shareholders. But of course, it will take time.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I can understand why the market has been pessimistic. To be honest with you, I underestimated how difficult market conditions would be. Fast-forward to today, however, and things are looking up. Yes, we may see some weakness for a good part of this year. That could lead to some short-term downside. But even if we just reach a point of stabilization and especially if we see management achieve additional cost savings, the end result could be a net positive for shareholders. This is especially true when you consider how cheap the stock is on both an absolute basis and relative to similar firms. Because of this, I'm going to keep the company rated a soft ‘buy’ for now.
